topic: Area of Study 3: Developments in Instrumental Jazz 1910 to Present
subject: music

items.correct: A recurring main theme (A) alternates with contrasting episodes (B, C, etc.).
items.misconception: A round where voices enter at different times.
items.why: Students confuse rondo (alternating refrain) with round (canon). Rondo: ABACA, ABACABA, etc. The A theme keeps coming back, like a refrain.

items.q: What three notes form a major triad in root position?
items.why: Students think 'three notes = a triad'. Triads are specific: stacked thirds from the root. C major = C, E, G (skipping D and F). Consecutive scale notes are clusters, not triads.

items.q: What is a balanced antecedent-consequent phrase?
items.correct: Two phrases of equal length where the first sounds open (question) and the second resolves (answer).
items.misconception: Any two phrases of similar style.
items.random_wrong: A long melody with no breaks.
items.why: Students miss the FUNCTIONAL relationship. The antecedent typically ends on an imperfect cadence (open); the consequent answers with a perfect cadence (closed). Length matches; cadences contrast.

items.q: Which Miles Davis album popularised modal jazz?
items.why: Correct answer: Kind of Blue. “Bitches Brew” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which saxophonist is most associated with the development of bebop?
items.why: Correct answer: Charlie Parker. “John Coltrane” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year was Miles Davis's 'Kind of Blue' released?
items.why: The real figure is 1959. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
